title: Use water like it can run out.
norm: Close running taps, fix leaks, reuse where you can, and harvest rain - India is among the most water-stressed countries, and every litre saved counts.
wit: The tap left running is the one nobody remembers opening.
goesRight: When people use water carefully, wells and rivers refill faster than they are drawn down - enough to drink, to grow food, even to cool power plants when the rains come late.

impact.cause: Using and wasting more water than a place can renew
impact.mechanism: Withdrawals outpace how fast groundwater and rivers recharge, so the shared supply shrinks - and in a stressed grid even power plants can't get enough cooling water
impact.effect: Shortages for homes and farms, and lost electricity when plants must cut output
impact.source_title: 25 Countries, Housing One-Quarter of the Population, Face Extremely High Water Stress
impact.supported_by: a lack of water to cool thermal powerplants between 2017 and 2021 resulted in 8.2 terawatt-hours in lost energy
impact.caveat: A WRI Aqueduct analysis for India; it shows how water scarcity already disrupts even electricity generation.

impact.cause: Living beyond the water a place can renew
impact.mechanism: When demand routinely uses up the available supply, communities run dry for parts of the year
impact.effect: Billions already face at least seasonal water shortage; India, being large and dry, is among the most exposed
impact.source_title: 25 Countries, Housing One-Quarter of the Population, Face Extremely High Water Stress
impact.supported_by: at least 50% of the world's population — around 4 billion people — live under highly water-stressed conditions for at least one month of the year
impact.caveat: A global figure; India is one of the largest and most water-stressed nations, so the pressure is felt sharply here.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: Switch off what you're not using.
norm: Turn off lights, fans, screens, and chargers when you leave - the cheapest, cleanest unit of power is the one nobody had to make.
wit: The fan cooling an empty room is just spinning for the walls.
goesRight: Switch off what's idle and total demand drops, so fewer coal units run - less CO2, less cooling water burned, and a smaller bill.

impact.cause: Leaving lights and appliances on when no one needs them
impact.mechanism: Idle devices still pull power, adding to the demand the grid has to generate
impact.effect: Wasted electricity and a higher bill - avoidable by the simple habit of switching off
impact.supported_by: The simplest and easiest way to save energy is to turn lights off when you leave a room.

impact.cause: More electricity demand than we actually need
impact.mechanism: Most of India's power still comes from burning coal, so extra demand is met largely by running more coal plants - which emit CO2 and drink cooling water
impact.effect: Avoidable coal burning; each unit saved cuts emissions and water use
impact.supported_by: Coal power generation – which makes up nearly three-quarters of coal demand in India – grew by 5% in 2024 mirroring growth in electricity demand.
impact.caveat: IEA notes coal is about three-quarters of India's coal demand and grew with electricity demand; treated here as a mechanism, not a precise generation share.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: Old phone? Recycle it, don't dump it.
norm: Hand old phones, chargers, batteries, and appliances to an authorised recycler - never dump or burn them. They hold both poisons and precious metals.
wit: Your drawer of dead chargers is a tiny, toxic goldmine.
goesRight: Proper recycling pulls out valuable metals and keeps lead, mercury, and other poisons out of the air, soil, and children's bodies.

impact.cause: Electronics that are never safely collected and recycled
impact.mechanism: Most e-waste is dumped or informally broken up rather than formally recycled, so its metals are lost and its toxins are freed
impact.effect: Growing mountains of e-waste, with only a small share handled safely
impact.supported_by: In 2022, an estimated 62 million tonnes of e-waste were produced globally. Only 22.3% was documented as formally collected and recycled.
impact.caveat: A global WHO figure for 2022; most e-waste worldwide is still not safely recycled.

impact.cause: Informal recycling, dumping, or open burning of electronics
impact.mechanism: Breaking and burning devices releases lead, mercury, dioxins, and many other chemicals into air, soil, and water
impact.effect: Harm to health, with children and pregnant women most at risk
impact.supported_by: Lead is a common substance released into the environment when e-waste is recycled, stored or dumped using informal activities, including open burning.
impact.caveat: A qualitative hazard; WHO notes children and pregnant women are especially vulnerable.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: Even AI has a footprint.
norm: Welcome data centres and AI - and build them with efficient cooling, clean power, and away from water-scarce places. A country can gain the technology without spending its rivers.
wit: Every 'quick question' to an AI runs on a machine that's very much awake, somewhere.
goesRight: When new data centres use efficient cooling and clean electricity, we get AI and digital services while keeping our power and water for people, farms, and nature.

impact.cause: The fast growth of data centres and AI computing
impact.mechanism: Every AI query and stored file runs on servers pulling real electricity from the grid
impact.effect: A fast-growing, measurable slice of the world's electricity - set to roughly double by 2030
impact.source_title: Energy and AI - Energy demand from AI
impact.supported_by: electricity consumption from data centres is estimated to amount to around 415 terawatt hours (TWh), or about 1.5% of global electricity consumption in 2024.
impact.caveat: A global IEA figure; there is no verified India-specific data-centre number, so for India this is the mechanism, not a local statistic.

impact.cause: How a data centre is cooled, and where it is built
impact.mechanism: Keeping servers cool takes energy and, in many designs, water; efficient designs need far less than wasteful ones
impact.effect: Efficient cooling and clean power sharply cut the electricity and water each data centre spends
impact.source_title: Energy and AI - Energy demand from AI
impact.supported_by: The share of cooling systems in total data centre consumption varies from about 7% for efficient hyperscale data centres to over 30% for less-efficient enterprise data centres.
impact.caveat: This is cooling's share of electricity, not water directly; efficient design cuts it from over 30% to about 7%, which also lowers water use.

impact.mechanism: Meeting today's wants by spending the water, clean air, and resources future generations will need is, by definition, not sustainable
impact.effect: The test for AI, industry, and every new technology: take the benefit without robbing the future
impact.supported_by: meeting the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs
impact.caveat: The Brundtland / UN definition of sustainable development - a guiding principle, not a statistic.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: Take what you'll eat. Finish what you take.
norm: Serve a little and go back for more, rather than piling a plate and binning half. Wasted food throws away all the water, land and work that grew it.
wit: The buffet is not a competition, and your plate is not a scoreboard.
affects: the water and land that grew it
someone who went hungry that day
goesRight: A plate finished, or leftovers saved for later, honours everything it took to grow the food - and the person who had none.

impact.cause: Avoidable household and consumer food waste
impact.mechanism: Growing food consumes water, land and energy that are all wasted when it is thrown away; food sent to landfill rots without air and releases methane, a potent greenhouse gas
impact.effect: Wasted natural resources and greenhouse-gas emissions, while food that could have fed people is lost

magnitude.unit: billion tonnes of edible food lost and wasted each year

impact.source_title: Understanding Food Loss and Waste - Why Are We Losing and Wasting Food?
impact.supported_by: approximately one-third of all produced foods (1.3 billion tons of edible food) for human consumption is lost and wasted every year
impact.caveat: A widely-cited global FAO estimate, shown for scale rather than as an Indian figure; food waste in landfill also emits methane, a greenhouse gas far more warming than carbon dioxide.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.
